title: Blaming the Court Over Reservation?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil reading copy below follows the source-verified chapter text. Obvious OCR spacing and line-break errors are repaired only in this derived translation file; the canonical Tamil source remains unchanged. English passages quoted in the printed letter are retained, followed by Kalaignar's Tamil explanation in translation.
paragraphs: On Anna Salai in Chennai, during the DMK government, a complex bearing Omandurar's name was established and a building was constructed for the Legislative Assembly and the Secretariat. Jayalalithaa changed both its intended purpose and its name, and announced that she would open a multi-super-speciality hospital there. On 27 December 2013, the Medical Services Recruitment Board issued an advertisement to select about one hundred senior officers to work in that hospital.

The advertisement stated that these appointments would be made on a contract basis and that there would be no reservation whatsoever. As soon as it appeared, most political parties in Tamil Nadu issued statements condemning it. But on 12 January 2014, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa released a reply from Kodanad. Even in that statement, instead of answering the others, she singled me out for an indecent attack and abused me in her characteristically uncivil manner. Nevertheless, because she is, after all, the Chief Minister, I gave her reply the respect due to it and answered it in detail on 13 January 2014, at a meeting held at Periyar Thidal in Chennai under the chairmanship of Tamil leader K. Veeramani.

Jayalalithaa had said that reservation was not being followed because of the Supreme Court judgment delivered on 18 July 2013, and that the present Union Government would certainly not bring the necessary amendment to the Constitution of India.

The Supreme Court delivered its judgment only on 18 July 2013. Once it was delivered, Parliament held an extensive debate on the matter on 13 August 2013. At that time, Union Law Minister Kapil Sibal assured the House that "a review petition challenging this judgment will be filed immediately on behalf of the Union Government." He also said that, "if necessary, even a constitutional amendment will be brought forward." Following that assurance, the Union Government filed a review petition in the Supreme Court. Yet Jayalalithaa has complained that the Union Government did nothing at all.
Moreover, notwithstanding the Supreme Court judgment, the Union Health Ministry stated in its communication that reservation must be followed in future appointments.

From this, I pointed out that the Union Government had not used the Supreme Court judgment as an excuse to interfere with reservation, and that it was Jayalalithaa who had taken shelter behind that judgment and acted against the principle of social justice. I also put several questions to her. On 22 November 1993, did Jayalalithaa's government give an undertaking before the Supreme Court that it would not implement 69 per cent reservation and would implement only 50 per cent, or did it not?
Did Jayalalithaa give an interview to the Indian Express saying that caste should not be the basis for reservation and that the criterion should instead be economic status, or did she not?

In that interview, did she not say, in a manner that would demolish the very foundation of social justice: "Some among the backward classes are well placed. Their receiving the benefit of reservation would make a mockery of the reservation policy itself. Benefits should therefore be made available to the economically weaker sections among the backward classes. Only then can it be called social justice. When the Union Government has decided on 27 per cent for the backward classes, there is nothing wrong in providing 5 or 10 per cent reservation even to the economically weaker sections among the forward classes"? Did she say this, or did she not?

These were among the questions I asked at the Periyar Thidal meeting. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has not answered a single one of them to this day. She could answer only if an answer were available to her, could she not? That is why, unable to reply, she flies into a rage and claws at me.

Meanwhile, when the review petition filed by the Union Government came up for hearing on 16 January 2014, the five judges of the Supreme Court declared: "It is for the Centre to take a decision whether there should be reservation in super-speciality Posts." In other words, the Union Government may decide whether reservation should be provided in super-speciality posts. During the hearing, Additional Solicitors General L. Nageswara Rao and K. V. Viswanathan, appearing for the Union Government, argued: "Indira Sawhney Judgment did not restrain the Government from providing Reservation in Super-Speciality Faculty Posts." That is, the Supreme Court judgment in the Indra Sawhney case did not prohibit the Government from providing reservation in super-speciality posts. Last year, citing from the Indra Sawhney judgment that accepted and granted 27 per cent reservation to backward classes in Union Government employment, the Supreme Court had observed: "In certain services in respect of certain posts, application of rule of reservation may not be advisable in regard to various technical posts, including posts in super-speciality medicine, engineering and other scientific and technical posts." In other words, it may not be advisable to provide reservation in certain super-speciality posts connected with medicine, engineering, science and technology. It must be remembered that this was not a VERDICT, but an OBSERVATION.

Reporting this development on the 17th, the Times of India wrote: "This will open the window for the Government to provide Reservation as a criteria for appointment to AIIMS Faculty Positions." That is, a window has been opened for the Government to use reservation as a criterion when making appointments to super-speciality faculty posts at the All India Institute of Medical Sciences.

Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has claimed that the Supreme Court judgment was the reason reservation was not followed in appointments to super-speciality posts. But the Supreme Court has now stated categorically in its judgment that the decision on following reservation rests with the Union Government. After saying that it could not recommend that reservation be followed, the Supreme Court also made clear that the "ball" is now in the Union Government's court. The Union Government's lawyers, too, have stated that the Supreme Court judgment did not prohibit reservation in such senior posts. Therefore, Jayalalithaa's claim that reservation was not followed in Tamil Nadu because of the Supreme Court judgment is nothing but a device to deceive the people and bury social justice.

After the Supreme Court delivered its judgment on 18 July 2013, the Union Government did not cite it to deny reservation when Puducherry's JIPMER Hospital and Medical College issued a notification on 31 July to recruit 114 medical officers. Posts were reserved for backward and Scheduled communities in accordance with the reservation policy. Reservation has continued even at Delhi's AIIMS after the Supreme Court judgment. Since a review petition had been filed against the judgment, the Union Health Ministry directed that reservation continue to be implemented; on that basis, 148 assistant professor posts were filled in accordance with reservation. Thus, even while reservation continued to be implemented in Union Government institutions after the Supreme Court judgment, the Tamil Nadu Government refused to implement it and blamed both the Supreme Court and the Union Government. The Tamil people must understand what a fraudulent trick this is.

Further, in her statement dated 12 January 2014, Jayalalithaa wrote: "I wish to inform Mr Karunanidhi that action is being taken to appoint doctors to the Omandurar Super-Speciality Hospital after considering the rules and precedents currently in force." Jayalalithaa says that selections are now being made without reservation only after considering the existing rules and precedents. But in the notification issued on 31 March 2013 by the Tamil Nadu Medical Services Recruitment Board, it was clearly stated: "The Rule of Reservation is applicable as per the Rules in force." Yet in the notification issued by the very same Board on 27 December 2013, this had been changed to: "There is no Reservation for these posts." And still, in her statement, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has told the barefaced lie that the notification was issued after considering the existing rules and precedents.

Next, I asked why the monthly salaries for the various doctors now being recruited had been fixed at such high levels as two and a half lakh rupees, one and a half lakh rupees, and one lakh rupees. Jayalalithaa replied in her characteristically high-handed fashion that the salaries were at this level because they would be working in a multi-super-speciality hospital. But do you know what monthly salary the government notification of 31 March 2013 prescribed for doctors? It stated: "Scale of Pay: Rs. 15,600 - 39,100 + Grade Pay 5,400/-." Why was a salary in the thousands raised to one in the lakhs? For what reason? How? It is enough if fair-minded people understand the difference between the two!

Condemning the Jayalalithaa government for acting against the policy of reservation, the DMK Students' Wing will hold a protest in Chennai on 21 January under the leadership of the State Students' Wing Secretary, younger brother Cuddalore Ila. Pugazhenthi. South Chennai District DMK Secretary, younger brother J. Anbazhagan, MLA, and Students' Wing Deputy Secretaries, younger brothers Govi. Chezhian, MLA, Kuthalam K. Anbazhagan, Coimbatore Ganesh Kumar, Poovai C. Gerald and Madurai K. Magizhan will preside alongside him.

It has been announced that the Dravidar Kazhagam Students' Wing will also participate in this protest. Bearing in mind that reservation is a foundational principle of the Dravidian movements, we must make the protest on the 21st a success. I expect that, at least now, those in this government will have a change of heart and announce that the policy of reservation will be followed in the notified recruitment of doctors. If, instead, they stubbornly insist that the rabbit they caught has only three legs, they will have to face the consequences!
With affection,
M. K.
20 January 2014
